title: Handling Middleware-Induced Route Conflicts in Express

statement.markdown: You are given an Express.js application that handles GET requests to the '/api/users' and '/api/users/:id' routes. However, the application experiences a middleware-induced route conflict when a GET request is made to '/api/users/id'. The conflict arises due to the incorrect order of route middleware. Your task is to fix the bug by reordering the middleware to prevent the conflict and ensure the application responds correctly to requests to both '/api/users' and '/api/users/:id'.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a string representing a path to be processed by the Express router.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a boolean indicating whether the request was handled successfully.
statement.notes: When handling the '/api/users/id' request, the application should respond with a 404 status code if the user ID is not found. If the user ID is found, the application should return the user data in JSON format.

constraints.description: Maximum number of user IDs to be processed

examples.explanation: The GET request to '/api/users' is handled successfully.
The GET request to '/api/users/1' is handled successfully and returns the user data in JSON format.
The GET request to '/api/users/non-existent-id' is not handled successfully and returns a 404 status code.

hints.text: Review the Express.js documentation for route middleware ordering best practices.
Consider using the 'express.Router' method to handle route conflicts.

editorial.title: Handling Middleware-Induced Route Conflicts in Express
editorial.intuition: The key to resolving the route conflict is to reorder the middleware to ensure that the '/api/users/:id' route is handled before the '/api/users' route.
editorial.bruteForce: One approach to resolving the conflict is to add a custom middleware function that checks if the request path matches '/api/users/:id'. If it does, the middleware function can handle the request and prevent the conflict.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using the 'express.Router' method to create a separate router for the '/api/users/:id' route. This allows us to handle the route conflict by prioritizing the custom router over the main router.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves creating a custom router for the '/api/users/:id' route and using the 'express.Router' method to prioritize the custom router over the main router. This ensures that the '/api/users/:id' route is handled correctly and prevents the route conflict.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct solution can be verified by sending GET requests to both '/api/users' and '/api/users/:id' and checking that the application responds correctly to each request.
editorial.dryRun: To test the solution, create an Express.js application and add the custom router for the '/api/users/:id' route. Use the 'express.Router' method to prioritize the custom router over the main router. Send GET requests to both '/api/users' and '/api/users/:id' and verify that the application responds correctly to each request.
commonMistakes: Failing to use the 'express.Router' method to handle route conflicts

starterCode.javascript: // Define the Express.js application and the custom router for the '/api/users/:id' route.
